quote: Is there anyone else on the title of the home with you?
stewart_read: Clean, correctly placed title check. Dawson confirms sole ownership, which eliminates spouse_decision protocol risk entirely. Carter executed this verify item without mechanical box-checking — it landed as a natural question in the flow.
coaching_implication: Positive execution — worth naming in the 1-on-1 as an example of the verify flow done right. No action needed here beyond reinforcement.

quote: And then what electric company are you working with?
stewart_read: Utility company captured cleanly (Rappahannock Electric). Carter also confirmed the name when Dawson abbreviated it ('RAP Electric' → 'Rappahannock?'). Competent verify execution.
coaching_implication: Positive execution. Pair with the title check at 02:52 when reviewing the verify section with the rep — these two items show the rep can run the verify flow naturally when not distracted by the address problem.

quote: Yeah. Still should be a a great fit. Not, like, the highest bill, but I think we can definitely still save you some money.
stewart_read: Carter captures the $120 bill and immediately hedges it ('not the highest bill'). The bill is acknowledged and dropped — not flipped. The codex's capture-and-flip pattern requires turning the bill into emotional alignment against the utility. Carter instead pre-emptively softened the pitch, which signals to Dawson that his situation is marginal rather than compelling. Reactive read holds: this is a missed flip, not a deliberate deferral.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Carter may have been managing expectations to avoid overselling a modest bill. Rejected — there is no follow-up move that uses the bill as leverage anywhere in the call.
coaching_implication: Show the rep the codex's objection_inversion triggers. The move here is to redirect the $120 toward what it represents over time on a brand-new home with five mini-splits — not to apologize for the number. Suggested direction (not a script): 'You've got five mini-splits and you just moved in — the design will show you what your actual usage looks like across a full seasonal cycle. That's exactly what the specialist maps out.' No fabricated projections; the curiosity is in the unknown, not a claimed number.

quote: I have five mini splits in the house.
stewart_read: Five mini-splits on a brand-new home is a material load signal. Dawson volunteers this unprompted, which means he is already thinking about energy consumption. Carter acknowledges it ('Okay. Gotcha.') and moves on without anchoring it. This is the strongest enthusiasm signal in the call — a customer who has already installed five electric HVAC units is telling you his electric load is real and growing.
coaching_implication: The manager should replay this moment and ask the rep: 'What does five mini-splits tell you about this customer's energy situation?' The rep should be able to articulate that this is a high-load home that hasn't yet been through a full seasonal cycle. The coaching move is curiosity_over_features — not explaining mini-split efficiency, but using the customer's own disclosure to create urgency around seeing the actual design numbers.

quote: Okay. Yeah. It would just be the it'd be the kilowatt usage. Mhmm. That's what it'd be. So the bar graph on the bill. Because the cost doesn't necessarily show us the the kilowatt usage.
stewart_read: Carter correctly distinguishes dollar amount from kilowatt-hour usage and directs Dawson to the bar graph on the bill. This is competent bill-collection execution — the rep knows what the design team actually needs and explains it clearly without over-complicating it.
coaching_implication: Positive execution. Worth noting in the 1-on-1 alongside the bill-anchor miss at 03:43 — the rep understands the technical requirement but missed the emotional opportunity. The gap is not knowledge; it is the flip.

quote: I mean, I would need the the picture of the roof and then the utility bill before I can put you in the schedule.
stewart_read: Carter gates the appointment on two customer-delivered assets without (a) locking a deadline for delivery, (b) confirming which roof-photo method Dawson will use, or (c) sending a confirmation text that would anchor the 9:30 AM slot. The button_up protocol requires appointment datetime confirmed, one-hour block stated, phone appointment specified, no-driving-at-computer anchor, and 'is that fair' handshake. None of these were executed. The 9:30 AM slot was verbally floated but never confirmed.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Carter may have been correctly holding the booking until assets arrive to avoid a no-show on an unqualified design. Partially valid — but the correct execution is to lock the time slot AND set a same-day asset deadline, not leave both open.
coaching_implication: Walk the rep through the full button_up checklist against this call. The rep should be able to say: 'I have you penciled for 9:30 tomorrow — send me the bill and the roof photo by end of day today and I'll send you the confirmation. Is that fair?' That locks the slot, sets the deadline, and gets the verbal handshake. As-is, the closer has no confirmed appointment to work from.

stewart_read: Dawson volunteers south-facing orientation before Carter asks — he has already assessed his home's solar suitability. Carter responds correctly ('It should be a great fit then') but does not anchor this as a customer-generated insight that makes the design appointment more valuable. The customer is doing the setter's curiosity-creation work for them.
coaching_implication: The manager should highlight this as a moment where the customer's own knowledge could have been reflected back to deepen commitment: 'You already know your roof faces south — that's exactly the kind of detail that makes the design worth seeing. Most people don't know that going in.' Per codex curiosity_over_features, the rep's job is to make the customer curious about their specific numbers, and the customer handed them the hook.

quote: Alright, Carter. I'll see and you'll be visiting the house tomorrow?
stewart_read: Dawson's closing question reveals he believes the appointment is an in-person visit — Carter has not clarified that this is a phone appointment with a specialist, not a site visit. Carter corrects this at 06:26 ('It won't be me. It'll be a a specialist'), but the correction comes after the call is effectively over and no button_up elements have been completed. The customer is going into the appointment with a mismatched expectation about format.
coaching_implication: This is a direct consequence of skipping the button_up protocol. The 'phone appointment, at your computer, not driving' anchor exists precisely to prevent this confusion. The manager should use this moment to show the rep what a missed button_up costs: the customer expected a house visit, not a phone call. If Dawson picks up tomorrow expecting someone at his door, the sit is at risk.
